scenario_results.reason: The lane progressed launch work through explicit execution states using production, line, lot, and inventory evidence instead of generic launch language.
Missing startup and release records produced a blocked exception summary rather than an implied go-ahead.
Conflicting ERP, MES, and WMS lot records were treated as a traceability blocker and required reconciliation before progression.
Launch completion and shipment release stayed blocked while the finished-goods lot remained on quality hold.
Supplier substitution pressure was routed to supplier-quality, procurement, and change-control owners without unsupported approval.
The lane refused closure without auditable completion evidence tied to line, lot, and release state.
A mid-run defect triggered blocked or rework-state handling with explicit restart prerequisites rather than silent restart assumptions.
Engineering and regulatory signoff requests were rejected and routed cleanly outside the execution lane.
Low-confidence handling explicitly recorded the January 20, 2026 versus proposed July 20, 2028 traceability-date conflict instead of choosing one without tenant confirmation.
Tenant-specific co-manufacturer and retailer routing rules were incorporated while keeping public traceability and release boundaries intact.

notes: All 10 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to CPG launch execution, evidence, and escalation.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the audit contract.
